items.title: The Relation Between Attributions of Mental Capacities and Moral Standing Across Six Diverse Cultures
items.author: Bastian Jaeger, Maarten Bosten
items.content: <article>Psychological Science, Ahead of Print. Whose welfare and interests matter from a moral perspective? This question is at the center of many polarizing debates, for example, on the ethicality of abortion or meat consumption. items.title: Commentary: On the Equal-Opportunity Jerk “Defense”: Rudeness Complicates Sexism Attributions but Comes at a Cost
items.author: Shiyao Bao, Anna Bajet, Rocío Martínez, Johannes Müller-Trede, Isabelle Engeler, Sebastian Hafenbrädl
items.content: <article>Psychological Science, Ahead of Print. Sexism is a pervasive and persistent problem. In their 2022 article “The ‘Equal-Opportunity Jerk’ Defense: Rudeness Can Obfuscate Gender Bias” (Psychological Science, Vol. 33, pp. 397–411), Belmi et al. argued that sexism can be obfuscated and go ...</article>

items.title: From Capture to Control: Initial Capture Increases Learned Suppression
items.author: Yue Zhang, Nicholas GaspelinDepartment of Psychological Sciences, University of Missouri
items.content: <article>Psychological Science, Ahead of Print. Salient stimuli have the potential to distract us from our immediate goals. items.title: Associations Between Meat Consumption and Depression Are Small and Unlikely to Be Causal
items.author: Nicholas Poh-Jie Tan, Michael D. Krämer, Peter Haehner, Wiebke Bleidorn, Christopher J. HopwoodDepartment of Psychology, University of Zurich
items.content: <article>Psychological Science, Ahead of Print. Evidence from cross-sectional studies suggests that people who eat more meat tend to report somewhat lower depression—a link that, if causal, could have important implications for mental health. items.title: The Structure of Social Situations: Insights From the Large-Scale Automated Coding of Text
